The workload issues a high number of small I/O operations (384 IOPS with 8 KB average size) resulting in very low bandwidth utilization (1.5% of peak) despite spending 84% of time in I/O. CPU utilization is low, indicating tasks are stalled on I/O. This pattern is typical of metadata contention on the shared NFS storage, where many small file operations overwhelm the metadata server.

The workload issues a very high number of small 2 KB read operations (921 IOPS) resulting in extremely low bandwidth utilization (0.9% of peak). Despite 84% of time spent in I/O, the low CPU utilization and bandwidth indicate the bottleneck is metadata server overload on the shared NFS storage, not data transfer.

CPU utilization is low, showing tasks are stalled on I/O metadata operations.

Despite spending 86% of time in I/O, bandwidth utilization is near zero, and CPU utilization is very low, indicating tasks are stalled waiting on metadata server responses rather than data transfer.

128 tasks writing in parallel to only 2 shared output files with 64 writers per file causes heavy lock contention, limiting write bandwidth to 22% of the 500 MB/s Lustre peak. CPU utilization at 20% and high I/O time ratio indicate tasks are stalled waiting for lock release rather than compute. The high sequential ratio and moderate average IO size rule out metadata or bandwidth saturation bottlenecks.

300 tasks concurrently writing to a single shared output file causes heavy lock contention, severely limiting write bandwidth to 19% of peak despite high parallelism. The low CPU utilization and high I/O time ratio indicate tasks are stalled waiting for lock access rather than compute or bandwidth saturation.

64 writers contend on a single shared output file, causing serialization of write access and limiting bandwidth to 21% of the GPFS peak. Despite high parallelism and moderate CPU utilization, the low bandwidth utilization indicates tasks are stalled waiting for write locks rather than compute or network. This lock contention bottleneck reduces effective I/O throughput.

The workflow reads from S3 with a high network utilization ratio of 95%, indicating the network is saturated. Despite a large peak storage bandwidth (1000 MB/s), the read bandwidth is only 95 MB/s, matching the network bandwidth, showing the network limits data transfer. CPU utilization is moderate, confirming the bottleneck is network I/O, not compute or storage device bandwidth.

The network utilization is at 95% of its peak bandwidth (190/200 MB/s), while storage bandwidth utilization is only 10% of the peak (190/2000 MB/s). This indicates that the network is the limiting factor in data transfer. CPU utilization is moderate, and I/O time dominates, confirming tasks are stalled on network I/O rather than compute or storage bandwidth.

The workflow reads from S3 with very low storage bandwidth utilization (9%) despite high read bandwidth (855 MB/s) matching network bandwidth (95% utilization). This indicates the network is the limiting factor in data transfer from remote object storage, causing I/O stalls and high io_time_ratio.

CPU utilization at 18% supports that compute is not the bottleneck.

Despite 100 tasks and high parallelism in execution, the I/O is serialized through a single writer (io_parallelism=1), severely limiting bandwidth to 48 MB/s (12% of peak). Low CPU utilization indicates tasks are stalled on I/O, confirming that lack of parallel I/O is the primary bottleneck.

The stage spends over half its time in I/O with low bandwidth utilization (12% of 400 MB/s peak) and low CPU usage, indicating I/O stalls. Frequent large checkpoints (12 checkpoints of 8 GB each) cause periodic heavy write I/O, dominating the I/O time and limiting overall write bandwidth. This checkpointing overhead is the primary bottleneck.

This pattern matches checkpointing overhead as the primary bottleneck.
